22: When Joram asks Jehu hašālôm yēhûʾ (‘is it šālôm, Jehu?’), Jehu’s response — mah haššālôm ʿaḏ zᵉnûnê ʾîzeḇel ʾimmᵉḵā, ‘what šālôm (peace) can there be, so long as the harlotries of your mother Jezebel and her sorceries are so many?’ — is the chapter’s fourth use of the šālôm-inquiry pattern (vv11, 17, 18, 19, 22). The repeated šālôm questions structure the chapter as a sequence of false-peace inquiries: the watchman asks, the king asks, the messengers ask — all using the covenant-greeting word — and in each case Jehu’s response is either a deflection or (here) an explicit denial. The ironic deployment of šālôm throughout the judgment narrative is prophetic rhetoric: the covenant-peace word (šālôm = wholeness, covenant-wellbeing) is exposed as an impossible category for the Ahab dynasty. Jeremiah 6:14 and 8:11 make explicit what the Jehu narrative implies: ‘they have healed the wound of my people lightly, saying, “Peace, peace,” when there is no peace.’ The false šālôm of the court prophets and the ironic šālôm-inquiries of 2 Kgs 9 are structurally identical: a peace-word used where YHWH has declared judgment. John 14:27 names the true šālôm’s source: ‘peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. Not as the world gives do I give to you.’ The šālôm the world cannot give (and the Ahab dynasty does not have) is the eschatological gift of the risen Christ.
1:
v1 — The young prophet sent to Ramoth-Gilead
Elisha summons one of the benei ha-neviim (sons of the prophets — the prophetic guild) and gives him a flask of oil and a commission. The word is shemen (oil), the element of royal and priestly consecration. The destination is Ramoth-Gilead, the frontier garrison where Israel and Aram have been contesting control (cf. 1 Kings 22; 2 Kings 8:28). Elisha does not go himself — the anonymous young prophet acts as deputy, and the haste of the commission ('hakhen motneicha' — gird your loins) signals the urgency of what follows.
2:
v2 — Find Jehu son of Jehoshaphat
The target is identified precisely: Yehu ben Yehoshafat ben Nimshi. The double patronymic (son of Jehoshaphat son of Nimshi) is unusual and likely signals that Jehu was already known to the narrative audience as the great soldier. He is to be separated from his companions and brought to an inner chamber (cheder be-cheder — lit. room within room), a phrase suggesting secrecy. The anointing is politically explosive and must be prepared covertly before announcement.
3:
v3 — Pour the oil and flee
The young prophet is told: pour the oil on his head, declare 'Thus says the LORD: I anoint you king over Israel,' then open the door and flee without delay (ve-nas'ta ve-lo techakeh — flee and do not wait). This compressed instruction highlights the dangerous nature of the commission — it is a revolutionary act performed in an occupied military zone while the sitting king (Joram) is recovering from battle wounds in Jezreel. The prophet's flight after anointing underscores that this is a word dropped like a spark into dry tinder.
4:
v4 — The young man goes to Ramoth-Gilead
The narrative is spare: the young prophet (literally 'the young man, the servant of the prophet') goes to Ramoth-Gilead and finds the army commanders sitting together. No dialogue is recorded; the text focuses on the act of obedience. The military setting — commanders seated, presumably in a war council — frames what follows as an act performed in the heart of Israel's military establishment.
5:
v5 — A word for you, commander
The prophet announces he has a word (davar) for Jehu specifically. Jehu asks 'For which of us?' (el-mi), and the prophet says 'For you, commander.' The form hassar (the commander/officer) frames Jehu's current status — he is still a military officer, not yet king. The transition from hassar to melech (king) is precisely what the next verses accomplish.
6:
v6 — The anointing oracle in the inner room
Brought inside, Jehu receives the oil poured on his head and the declaration: 'Koh amar YHWH Elohei Yisrael: meshachtika le-melech al-am YHWH al-Yisrael' — 'Thus says the LORD God of Israel: I anoint you king over the people of the LORD, over Israel.' The phrase am YHWH (people of the LORD) is significant — it frames the kingship as stewardship of a covenant people, not merely political authority. The anointing by oil makes this a sacred, not merely military, appointment.
7:
v7 — Strike down the house of Ahab
The mission is stated: destroy (hikita) the house of Ahab your master, so that I may avenge the blood of my servants the prophets and the blood of all the servants of the LORD from the hand of Jezebel. The term avenge is niqamti (I will avenge — 1st person divine), framing the purge as God's own justice administered through Jehu. Jezebel is named specifically as the one from whose hand God will exact blood-guilt — her role in the Baal persecution and prophetic deaths is the central charge.
8:
v8 — The whole house of Ahab shall perish
The oracle extends to every male in Ahab's house: kol-beit Achav will be cut off, both the confined and the free (atzur ve-azuv — a formulaic phrase for totality, covering all social categories). The same formula was used for the judgments against Jeroboam (1 Kings 14:10) and Baasha (1 Kings 16:11). Ahab's dynasty joins the pattern of dynastic elimination for apostasy that structures Kings throughout.
9:
v9 — Like the house of Jeroboam and the house of Baasha
The oracle makes the comparison explicit: the house of Ahab will be made like the house of Jeroboam son of Nebat and like the house of Baasha son of Ahijah. These two prior dynasties were each annihilated for leading Israel into sin. The pattern is now completing its third instance with Ahab's line. The Deuteronomic theology of Kings traces a consistent logic: dynasties that corrupt the covenant are cut off, often through violence commissioned by prophetic word.
10:
v10 — Dogs shall eat Jezebel
The oracle closes with the fate of Jezebel: 've-et Izevel yokhlu ha-kelavim be-chelkat Yizreel ve-ein qover' — 'The dogs shall eat Jezebel in the plot of Jezreel, and there shall be none to bury her.' No burial is the ultimate disgrace in the ancient world — to be eaten by dogs is to be denied all dignity in death. This directly fulfills Elijah's word in 1 Kings 21:23. The young prophet then opens the door and flees, as instructed.
11:
v11 — Is all well? Why did this madman come to you?
When Jehu returns to the other commanders, they ask about the encounter: ha-shalom (is it well / is there peace?) — the same word that will echo grimly in v17-19. They call the young prophet ha-meshugga (the madman/the mad one) — a term used for ecstatic prophets viewed with suspicion by the military establishment (cf. Jeremiah 29:26). The pejorative reveals the uneasy relationship between prophetic enthusiasm and military culture in Israel.
12:
v12 — Jehu reveals the anointing
When pressed, Jehu reveals the substance of what was said: koh ve-khoh diber elai — 'He spoke to me thus and thus.' He then recites the anointing oracle. The moment of disclosure is charged — these are the men who will either support or resist the revolution. Their response (v13) shows they immediately embrace it.
13:
v13 — Garments spread on the steps: instant acclamation
The commanders each took his garment and placed it beneath Jehu on the bare steps (ha-garem), blew the shofar (tiqe'u ba-shofar), and proclaimed 'Yehu melech' — 'Jehu is king.' The garment-spreading is a spontaneous royal gesture (cf. Matthew 21:8 — palms and cloaks for Jesus), here performed on a staircase rather than a throne room. The shofar proclamation in a military garrison marks this as a coup born of prophetic word and military consent simultaneously.
14:
v14 — Jehu conspires against Joram
The narrative provides a brief historical parenthesis: Joram (the king of Israel) had been at Ramoth-Gilead guarding against Aram with all Israel, because of Hazael king of Aram. Joram had gone to Jezreel to recover from wounds received in battle. The conspiratorial context (va-yiqshor Yehu ben Yehoshafat ben Nimshi el-Yoram) positions Jehu as a military officer turning on his own sovereign — authorized only by the prophetic commission just received.
15:
v15 — Seal Ramoth-Gilead; let no one escape to tell Jezreel
Jehu's first strategic move is information control: im yesh nafshechem al-yetze palit min-ha-ir lalekhet le-haggid be-Yizreel — 'If it is your wish, let no one slip out of the city to go and tell in Jezreel.' He then rides for Jezreel himself. The race against information is the drama of vv15-20 — whoever gets word to Joram first determines whether the coup succeeds or fails. Jehu's military experience shapes his tactical instincts.
16:
v16 — Jehu rides to Jezreel
Va-yirkav Yehu va-yelekh el-Yizreel — 'Jehu rode and went to Jezreel.' The forward motion begins. Joram king of Israel and Ahaziah king of Judah (who had come down to visit the convalescing Joram) are both in Jezreel — a detail that makes Jehu's arrival the convergence point for two royal fates. The narrative's pace accelerates.
17:
v17 — The watchman on the tower of Jezreel
The watchman (ha-tzofeh) on the tower sees the company of Jehu approaching and reports to Joram: 'I see a company.' Joram sends a horseman with the message ha-shalom — 'Is it peace?' This word shalom now becomes the hinge of the entire episode: asked three times (vv17, 18, 19, 22) with increasing irony, it exposes the impossibility of peace under Jezebel's house.
18:
v18 — The first messenger joins Jehu's company
The messenger reaches Jehu and delivers the king's question: koh amar ha-melech ha-shalom — 'Thus says the king: Is it peace?' Jehu dismisses him: 'Ma lecha ve-la-shalom suv el-achorai' — 'What do you have to do with peace? Turn behind me.' The messenger falls in behind Jehu, effectively captured by the coup without realizing it. The watchman reports to Joram that the messenger has reached the company but is not returning.
19:
v19 — The second messenger also joins Jehu
A second horseman is sent with the same question (ha-shalom), and Jehu gives the same dismissal: 'What do you have to do with peace? Turn behind me.' He too joins the company. The repeated pattern (two messengers sent, two absorbed) creates dramatic escalation and underscores that Joram's court cannot even receive news of what is happening — the coup is swallowing its own communications.
20:
v20 — The driving is like the driving of Jehu son of Nimshi
The watchman reports the approach: 'ha-nehigah ke-nihag Yehu ven Nimshi ki be-shigga'on yinhag' — 'The driving is like the driving of Jehu son of Nimshi — for he drives furiously.' The word shigga'on (madness/fury) echoes the earlier 'madman' label applied to the prophet — Jehu himself is characterized by dangerous intensity. This line became proverbial (the English phrase 'driving like Jehu' derives from it), capturing a reckless, unstoppable momentum.
21:
v21 — Joram goes out to meet Jehu
Joram commands his chariot harnessed and goes out with Ahaziah king of Judah to meet Jehu. They meet at the plot (chelkat) of Naboth the Jezreelite — the precise location is not accidental. The narrator has positioned the confrontation at the very piece of land Ahab and Jezebel seized by having Naboth murdered (1 Kings 21). Geography becomes theology: the reckoning arrives at the site of the original crime.
23:
v23 — Joram turns and flees: treachery, Ahaziah!
Joram recognizes what is happening — mirma (treachery/deceit) — and turns his chariot to flee, shouting to Ahaziah: 'Treachery, Ahaziah!' The cry is a warning but also a recognition of betrayal from within Israel's own military. The word mirma (from ramah — to deceive) frames Jehu's coup in Joram's terms as treachery, though the narrative frames it as divine commission.
24:
v24 — Jehu draws his bow and shoots Joram between the shoulders
Ve-Yehu male yado be-qeshet va-yak et-Yoram bein zero'otav — 'Jehu filled his hand with the bow and struck Joram between the arms [shoulders/scapulae].' The arrow goes through his heart (vayetze ha-chetz mi-libbo — the arrow went out through his heart). Joram sinks in his chariot. The execution is swift and precise — Jehu the military man kills with a soldier's accuracy.
25:
v25 — Throw him in the plot of Naboth
Jehu commands Bidkar his officer: 'Take him up and throw him in the plot (chelkat) of Naboth the Jezreelite.' Then he recites the word the LORD spoke against Ahab in the field of Naboth (1 Kings 21:19): 'Yesterday I saw the blood of Naboth and the blood of his sons.' The disposal site is chosen deliberately to complete a long-deferred justice. The narrator wants the reader to feel the precision: the same field, the same logic, 'paid back on this plot.'
26:
v26 — The blood of Naboth and his sons
The divine word recalled here goes beyond the MT of 1 Kings 21: Jehu references 'the blood of his sons' (dam banaiv) — sons not mentioned in the preserved Elijah oracle. This may reflect a fuller tradition about what happened to Naboth's family (killed along with him to ensure the land transfer was legally clean), or a variant tradition. Either way, the oracle's fulfillment encompasses an entire family's blood that has gone unanswered for years.
27:
v27 — Ahaziah king of Judah flees and is struck at the ascent of Gur
When Ahaziah king of Judah sees what is happening, he flees by way of the garden house (beit ha-gan). Jehu pursues and commands 'Strike him too' (oto gam-atkuhu). The blow falls at the ascent of Gur, near Ibleam. Ahaziah flees to Megiddo and dies there. His officials carry him to Jerusalem for burial — he receives a royal burial (unlike Joram and later Jezebel), presumably because he is a king of Judah and descended from David.
28:
v28 — Ahaziah buried in Jerusalem in the city of David
Ahaziah's body is transported from Megiddo to Jerusalem and buried in the tomb of his ancestors in the city of David. The Chronicler's parallel (2 Chronicles 22:9) differs in detail about where he died, but both traditions agree on the Jerusalem burial. Ahaziah's reign — briefly described in 2 Kings 8 — lasted only one year; his end here is entangled with the purge of Ahab's house through his maternal connection to Jezebel's bloodline.
29:
v29 — The eleventh year of Joram son of Ahab, Ahaziah began to reign
A synchronistic note anchors Ahaziah's accession: the eleventh year of Joram son of Ahab over Israel. This synchronism conflicts with the accession notice in 2 Kings 8:25 (twelfth year) — a well-known textual crux usually resolved by counting conventions (inclusive/exclusive reckoning). The note functions to tie Ahaziah's fate to Joram's as part of one historical moment, not two separate careers.
30:
v30 — Jezebel paints her eyes and adorns her head
When Jehu comes to Jezreel, Jezebel hears, paints her eyes with kohl (va-tasem ba-pukh eineiha), adorns her hair, and looks out through the window. The act has been interpreted variously: as seduction, as defiance, or as a queen choosing to meet death in full royal dignity rather than in terror. She is dying as a queen, not as a fugitive. Her verbal assault on Jehu from the window ('Is it peace, you Zimri, murderer of your master?') names him with the most shameful precedent — Zimri, who killed his king and lasted seven days (1 Kings 16:9-15).
31:
v31 — Is it peace, Zimri, killer of your master?
Jezebel's taunt invokes Zimri — the short-lived assassin-king who lasted one week before Omri swept him away. The comparison is calculated: Zimri killed his master Elah and took the throne, only to be immediately overwhelmed. Jezebel is suggesting that Jehu's rebellion will be equally short-lived. It is also possibly a legal accusation — the word for 'killer of his master' (horeg adonav) frames Jehu as a criminal, not a legitimate king. She dies with this challenge on her lips.
32:
v32 — Who is on my side? Throw her down
Jehu looks up at the window and calls: 'Mi iti mi' — 'Who is with me? Who?' Two or three eunuchs (sarisei) look out at him. He commands: 'Shifluha' — 'Throw her down.' The eunuchs' obedience is immediate. The palace servants are the instruments of her death — those within her own household turn against her. The eunuchs may have grievances of their own, or they may simply read the moment accurately.
33:
v33 — They threw her down and her blood spattered
They throw her down; her blood spatters (va-yiz) on the wall and on the horses; and the horses trampled her (va-yirmesuha). The scene is deliberately un-sanitized: the violence is graphic and total. This is not burial or honor — it is obliteration. The horses trampling her body completes the dehumanizing end that the oracle had promised. The fulfillment of Elijah's word has arrived.
34:
v34 — Go and bury this cursed woman
When Jehu goes in to eat and drink, he commands: 'See to this cursed woman (ha-arurah ha-zot) and bury her, for she is a king's daughter.' The phrase bat melech (daughter of a king) grants minimal acknowledgment of status — Jezebel was daughter of Ethbaal king of the Sidonians (1 Kings 16:31). Even in issuing a burial order, Jehu shows no grief or magnanimity; the concession to burial is merely a minimum owed to royal lineage.
35:
v35 — They found no more of her than skull, feet, and palms of hands
When they go to bury her, they find only the skull (gulgolet), feet (raglayim), and palms of the hands (kapot ha-yadayim). The dogs have consumed the rest. The scene answers the oracle precisely: nothing left to bury. The word gulgolet (skull) will recur at Golgotha — the skull-place — but here it marks the literal fulfillment of a prophetic curse.
36:
v36 — This is the word of the LORD that he spoke by Elijah
When they report to Jehu what was found, he declares: 'Zeh ha-davar asher diber YHWH be-yad Eliyahu ha-Tishbi' — 'This is the word which the LORD spoke by the hand of Elijah the Tishbite.' Then he recites the oracle from 1 Kings 21:23 almost verbatim: 'In the plot of Jezreel the dogs shall devour Jezebel's flesh.' The explicit citation of Elijah's name and oracle is a narrator's signature — this entire sequence has been the fulfillment of a word spoken years before. The text wants the reader to register the correlation.
37:
v37 — She shall be as dung on the face of the ground
The oracle continues: Jezebel's corpse shall be like dung (ke-domen) on the surface of the ground in the plot of Jezreel, so that none can say 'This is Jezebel.' The most feared aspect is erasure: no grave marker, no named resting place, no monument. In the ancient world where royal tombs were markers of ongoing dynasties, to die without burial is to be erased from memory. The oracle accomplishes theological and practical goals simultaneously — justice is visible, and the dynasty dies without a grave to rally around.
